articles.title: Update of guideline for diagnosis and treatment of subacromial pain syndrome: a multidisciplinary review by the Dutch Orthopedic Association Part 2: Operative considerations and treatment of various conditions related to subacromial pain syndrome.
articles.authors: Lambers Heerspink FO, Veen EJD, Dorrestijn O, Visser CPJ, Leijs MJC, Van Poppel D, et al.
articles.population: Patients with subacromial pain syndrome, including supraspinatus tears, biceps tendon pathology, and calcific tendinosis
articles.outcomes: Recommendations for operative and non-operative management of supraspinatus tears, biceps tendon pathology, and calcific tendinosis
articles.take_home: For isolated supraspinatus tears, start with exercise-based therapy and corticosteroid injection, and only consider surgery if symptoms do not improve after 3-6 months. For calcific tendinosis, try a procedure called barbotage first, reserving surgery for persistent large calcifications.

articles.title: Update of guideline for diagnosis and treatment of subacromial pain syndrome: a multidisciplinary review by the Dutch Orthopedic Association Part 1: preventive measures, diagnostics, and non-surgical treatment of subacromial pain syndrome.
articles.authors: Lambers Heerspink FO, Veen EJD, Dorrestijn O, Visser CPJ, Leijs MJC, Van Poppel D, et al.
articles.population: Patients with subacromial pain syndrome (non-traumatic, non-rheumatologic shoulder complaints with pain during arm elevation)
articles.outcomes: Diagnostic accuracy of physical tests, effectiveness of corticosteroid injection, barbotage for calcific tendinosis, suprascapular nerve block for therapy-resistant cases, and exercise-based management
articles.take_home: This guideline recommends using a cluster of physical tests to diagnose shoulder pain syndrome, and suggests corticosteroid injections or other procedures only when conservative measures like exercise therapy are not working well enough on their own. A healthy lifestyle and gradual exercise are the foundation of treatment.

articles.title: Reverse versus anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty in patients over 70 with a competent rotator cuff and glenohumeral osteoarthritis: a meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Gupta MS, Krishan A, Rashid A, Lee MH
articles.population: Patients aged 70 years and over with glenohumeral osteoarthritis and a functioning rotator cuff
articles.outcomes: Range of motion (external rotation, internal rotation, forward elevation, abduction), patient-reported function (ASES, Constant, SST scores), revision rates, complication rates, reoperation rates
articles.take_home: Anatomic shoulder replacement gives better arm rotation than reverse shoulder replacement in older patients with a good rotator cuff, but reverse replacement has fewer complications and revisions. The choice between them should depend on what matters most to the individual patient.

articles.title: Comparing shoulder surgery outcomes in workers' compensation versus nonworkers' compensation patients: a meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Parmar T, Daher M, Kermanshahi N, Berg J, Boufadel P, Fares MY, et al.
articles.population: Adults undergoing shoulder surgery, comparing workers' compensation patients with non-workers' compensation patients
articles.sample_size: 2676 patients across 28 studies (942 workers' compensation, 1734 non-workers' compensation)
articles.outcomes: Shoulder function scores, pain scores, complication rates, revision surgery rates, and return to work
articles.take_home: Workers' compensation patients had worse shoulder function and pain scores in the longer term and experienced more complications after surgery compared to non-workers' compensation patients, though they had similar revision rates and return to work outcomes. The reasons for these differences are unclear and may reflect the need for better support during recovery.

articles.title: Why do primary anatomic total shoulder arthroplasties fail today? A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Lauck BJ, Cathey JM, Adams NC, Reynolds AW, Trasolini NA, Waterman BR, et al.
articles.population: Patients who underwent primary anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty for glenohumeral osteoarthritis
articles.sample_size: 35,168 shoulder replacement procedures across 44 studies, with 2,744 failures
articles.outcomes: Causes of shoulder replacement failure including implant loosening, rotator cuff insufficiency, infection and instability
articles.take_home: Implant loosening, particularly of the glenoid (socket) component, is the most common reason why shoulder replacements fail, accounting for over a quarter of failures. Rotator cuff problems, infection and instability are also important causes of failure.

articles.title: Preoperative corticosteroid injections are associated with a higher periprosthetic infection rate following primary total shoulder arthroplasty: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Schoell K, Crabb R, Simpson E, Deshpande V, Gardner V, Quilligan E, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total shoulder arthroplasty who received corticosteroid injections before surgery
articles.outcomes: Periprosthetic joint infection (PJI) rate following shoulder arthroplasty
articles.take_home: Patients who had corticosteroid injections before shoulder surgery had a higher risk of infection after their operation, with the risk being higher when injections were given closer to the time of surgery. The overall infection rate was 5.4% for any prior injection, rising to 7.9% if injected within 3 months and 9.0% if injected within 1 month of surgery.

articles.title: Reverse versus anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty for glenohumeral osteoarthritis with intact cuff: a meta-analysis of clinical outcomes.
articles.authors: Daher M, Boufadel P, Fares MY, Lopez R, Goltz DE, Khan AZ, et al.
articles.population: Patients with glenohumeral osteoarthritis and an intact rotator cuff
articles.outcomes: Complications, reoperations, pain and disability scores, shoulder function scores, and range of motion
articles.take_home: Reverse shoulder replacement had fewer complications and reoperations than anatomic shoulder replacement for osteoarthritis with an intact rotator cuff, and patients reported better pain and function scores. However, reverse shoulder replacement resulted in reduced external rotation (outward arm movement).

articles.title: Bioinductive patch as an augmentation for rotator cuff repair, a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Warren JR, Domingo-Johnson ER, Sorensen AA, Cheng AL, Latz KH, Cil A
articles.population: People with full and partial-thickness rotator cuff tears undergoing surgery
articles.sample_size: 13 clinical trials included in the meta-analysis
articles.outcomes: Retear rate, pain, shoulder function scores, tendon thickness, complication rates, and patient-reported outcome measures
articles.take_home: A bioinductive collagen patch used during rotator cuff repair surgery appears safe and may reduce the risk of the repair tearing again compared to standard repair alone, with retear rates of 8.3% for full-thickness and 1.1% for partial-thickness tears. Patient improvements in pain, function and tendon thickness were similar to standard repair, though complication rates were comparable or slightly higher.

articles.title: International trends in shoulder replacement: a meta-analysis from 11 public joint registers.
articles.authors: Rupani N, Combescure C, Silman A, Lubbeke A, Rees J
articles.population: Patients undergoing shoulder replacement across 11 international public joint registries
articles.outcomes: Annual growth rates of shoulder replacements, trends in use of reverse total shoulder replacement (RTSR) versus anatomical total shoulder replacement (TSR) and hemiarthroplasty across different diagnoses, standardisation of patient-reported outcomes measures
articles.take_home: Shoulder replacements are being performed more frequently, with reverse total shoulder replacements almost doubling in use between 2011 and 2019. However, this increase in reverse replacements is happening without strong evidence to support their use for most conditions, except for cuff tear arthropathy where they are now standard.

articles.title: Factors associated with reparability of rotator cuff tears: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Hsu KL, Kuan FC, Velasquez Garcia A, Hong CK, Chen Y, Shih CA, et al.
articles.population: Patients with rotator cuff tears
articles.outcomes: Factors associated with whether rotator cuff tears can be surgically repaired, including age, tear size, fatty infiltration, muscle atrophy, and humeral head position
articles.take_home: Older age, larger tears, severe fatty infiltration, muscle atrophy and upward movement of the upper arm bone are strongly linked to rotator cuff tears that cannot be repaired. Simple imaging signs like the tangent sign can help predict whether a tear is repairable.

articles.title: Mid- to long-term outcomes of latissimus dorsi tendon transfer for massive irreparable posterosuperior rotator cuff tears: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Velasquez Garcia A, Nieboer MJ, de Marinis R, Morrey ME, Valenti P, Sanchez-Sotelo J
articles.population: Patients with massive irreparable posterosuperior rotator cuff tears treated with latissimus dorsi tendon transfer
articles.outcomes: Constant-Murley Score improved by 28 points, pain reduced by 2.5 points, forward flexion increased by 43 degrees, abduction by 38 degrees, external rotation by 8 degrees. Complication rate 13%, revision surgery rate 6%.
articles.take_home: Latissimus dorsi tendon transfer significantly improved shoulder function, pain, and movement in patients with massive irreparable rotator cuff tears at 4 to 20+ years follow-up. Complication and revision rates were modest at around 13% and 6% respectively.

articles.title: Bone channeling in arthroscopic rotator cuff repair: a systematic review and meta-analysis of level I studies.
articles.authors: Fairley JA, Pollock JW, McIlquham K, Lapner P
articles.population: Adults aged 18 years or over with full-thickness rotator cuff tears undergoing arthroscopic repair
articles.outcomes: Functional scores (Constant-Murley), retear rates, pain, and reoperation rates
articles.take_home: Adding bone marrow channeling to arthroscopic rotator cuff repair does not improve how well the shoulder works, reduce pain, lower the chance of the repair failing, or reduce the need for further surgery. The technique appears to offer no additional benefit compared to standard repair alone.

articles.title: Subscapularis repair for reverse shoulder arthroplasty: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Bethell MA, Hurley ET, Welch J, Cabell G, Levin J, Lassiter TE, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing reverse shoulder arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Shoulder instability, revision rates, complications, shoulder function scores (ASES and Constant scores), pain, and range of motion (forward flexion, internal rotation, external rotation, abduction)
articles.take_home: Repairing the subscapularis muscle during reverse shoulder surgery reduces instability compared to not repairing it, though this benefit is less clear when the implant is positioned differently. Function scores and some movements improved with repair, but the practical differences were small and did not reach clinically meaningful levels.

articles.title: Tranexamic acid use in arthroscopic rotator cuff repair is an effective and safe adjunct to improve visualization: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Han C, Liu M, Lian X, Sun T, Yan S, Bai X, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing arthroscopic rotator cuff repair surgery
articles.sample_size: 537 patients total (272 with tranexamic acid, 265 without)
articles.outcomes: Visual clarity during surgery, operative time, postoperative pain, blood loss, shoulder swelling, irrigation fluid volume, and adverse cardiovascular events
articles.take_home: Tranexamic acid improved the surgeon's ability to see during rotator cuff repair surgery and reduced operative time by about 11 minutes, with no reported adverse events in the studies reviewed.

articles.title: Association of obesity with high retears and complication rates, and low functional scores after rotator cuff repair: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Yang Z, Chen W, Liang J, Liu T, Zhang B, Wang X, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing rotator cuff repair surgery, comparing obese and non-obese patients
articles.outcomes: Retear rates, reoperation rates, complication rates, shoulder function scores, pain levels, and shoulder movement
articles.take_home: Obese patients who have rotator cuff repair surgery have higher rates of retearing, needing further surgery, and complications, as well as more pain and worse shoulder function after surgery compared to non-obese patients. Surgery duration and shoulder movement range were similar between the groups.

articles.title: Outcomes of subacromial balloon spacer implantation for irreparable rotator cuff tears: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Berk AN, Cregar WM, Gachigi KK, Trofa DP, Schiffern SC, Hamid N, et al.
articles.population: Adults with irreparable rotator cuff tears
articles.sample_size: 894 shoulders in 886 patients across 17 studies included in meta-analysis
articles.outcomes: Pain, shoulder function and movement (forward elevation, abduction, external rotation), postoperative complications including device-related events and need for further surgery
articles.take_home: Subacromial balloon spacer implantation appears safe and effective for irreparable rotator cuff tears, with patients showing improvements in pain and shoulder function in the short term. However, we do not yet know how this treatment compares to other options or how long the benefits last.

articles.title: Scaffold- and graft-based biological augmentation of rotator cuff repair: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis of preclinical and clinical studies for 2010-2022.
articles.authors: Mandalia K, Mousad A, Welborn B, Bono O, Le Breton S, MacAskill M, et al.
articles.population: People undergoing rotator cuff repair surgery
articles.sample_size: 62 studies reviewed (47 animal studies and 15 clinical studies in humans)
articles.outcomes: Retear rate, biomechanical strength, stiffness, load to failure, patient functional scores, complications
articles.take_home: Using biological scaffolds or grafts to reinforce rotator cuff repairs significantly reduces the chance of the repair failing again compared to repair without augmentation. Both animal and clinical studies show these techniques are safe and improve healing, with acellular human dermal allograft and bovine collagen showing the most promise.

articles.title: Effectiveness of abduction brace versus simple sling rehabilitation following rotator cuff repair: systematic review and meta-analyses.
articles.authors: Chen H, Wu S, Qiang H, Liu S
articles.population: Patients undergoing arthroscopic rotator cuff repair
articles.sample_size: 302 patients across 4 randomised controlled trials included in meta-analysis
articles.outcomes: Constant score, Western Ontario Rotator Cuff index, pain score, shoulder range of motion, and rotator cuff re-tearing rates
articles.take_home: Using an abduction brace after rotator cuff repair did not produce better results than using a simple sling for pain, shoulder movement, function or healing. A simple sling may be preferable because it is more cost effective.

articles.title: Chronic calcific tendonitis of the rotator cuff: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials comparing operative and nonoperative interventions.
articles.authors: Angileri HS, Gohal C, Comeau-Gauthier M, Owen MM, Shanmugaraj A, Terry MA, et al.
articles.population: People with chronic calcific tendonitis of the rotator cuff
articles.sample_size: 2352 patients total (2212 nonoperative, 140 operative) across 27 randomized controlled trials
articles.outcomes: Pain reduction on visual analog scale, shoulder function using Constant-Murley score, and complete resolution of calcium deposits on imaging
articles.take_home: Surgery produced better improvements in shoulder function than non-surgical treatments, though all approaches reduced pain and improved function. Needle treatment and shockwave therapy are reasonable first-line treatments, with surgery showing better functional results if conservative care fails.

articles.title: Comparison of the effects of platelet-rich plasma and corticosteroid injection in rotator cuff disease treatment: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Peng Y, Li F, Ding Y, Sun X, Wang G, Jia S, et al.
articles.population: People with rotator cuff disease
articles.outcomes: Shoulder function scores (Constant, Simple Shoulder Test, American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons scores) and pain reduction measured by visual analog scale at short-term, mid-term and long-term follow-up
articles.take_home: Corticosteroid injections work better in the short term, whilst platelet-rich plasma (PRP) shows better results in the long term, with no difference between the two in the medium term. However, the differences found were small and may not be clinically meaningful in practice.

articles.title: The clinical impact of retears after repair of posterosuperior rotator cuff tears: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Holtedahl R, Boe B, Brox JI
articles.population: Adults undergoing surgical repair of full-thickness posterosuperior rotator cuff tears
articles.sample_size: 3350 participants across 43 studies, median 65 per study
articles.outcomes: Shoulder pain and function measured by Constant-Murley score, American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons score, muscle strength, and health-related quality of life at median 18 months follow-up
articles.take_home: About one in four patients had a retear on imaging after rotator cuff repair, and whilst patients with retears had worse pain and function scores than those with healed repairs, these differences were small and most patients still had satisfactory outcomes regardless of whether the repair healed.

articles.title: Management of glenoid bone loss in primary reverse shoulder arthroplasty : a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Wilcox B, Campbell RJ, Low AK, Yeoh T
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary reverse shoulder arthroplasty with glenoid bone loss, mean age 72.2 years
articles.sample_size: 919 shoulders across 13 studies (292 with bone graft, 627 with augmented prostheses)
articles.outcomes: Range of motion, patient-reported functional outcomes (American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons Score), complication rates, revision rates
articles.take_home: Both bone grafting and augmented glenoid prostheses improve shoulder function and movement in reverse shoulder surgery for bone loss. Augmented prostheses had better functional scores and fewer complications and revisions than bone grafting.

articles.title: Total shoulder arthroplasty vs. hemiarthroplasty in patients with primary glenohumeral arthritis with intact rotator cuff: meta-analysis using the ratio of means.
articles.authors: Singh Jagdev B, McGrath J, Cole A, Gomaa AR, Chong HH, Singh HP
articles.population: Adults with primary glenohumeral arthritis and an intact rotator cuff
articles.outcomes: Patient-reported outcome measures, revision surgery rates, and complication rates
articles.take_home: Total shoulder arthroplasty produced slightly better patient-reported outcomes than hemiarthroplasty and had lower rates of needing further surgery. The combined analysis suggests total shoulder arthroplasty carries a lower overall risk of complications or revision compared to hemiarthroplasty in patients with intact rotator cuffs.

articles.title: Outcomes and complications of primary reverse shoulder arthroplasty with minimum of 2 years' follow-up: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Galvin JW, Kim R, Ment A, Durso J, Joslin PMN, Lemos JL, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary reverse shoulder arthroplasty for rotator cuff tear arthropathy, irreparable rotator cuff tears, glenoid deformity, fracture sequelae, and revision shoulder arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Active flexion improved by 56 degrees, active abduction by 50 degrees, active external rotation by 14 degrees. Constant score improved by 37 points and ASES score by 42 points. Overall complication rate 9.4%, revision rate 2.6%, infection rate 1.2%.
articles.take_home: Reverse shoulder replacement is a safe procedure with good outcomes, resulting in meaningful improvements in shoulder movement and function. Complications are relatively uncommon, occurring in about 1 in 10 patients.

articles.title: Does suprascapular nerve release provide additional benefits for rotator cuff repair: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Ma HH, Wu WT, Tsai IC, Chang KV
articles.population: Patients undergoing rotator cuff repair
articles.outcomes: Shoulder function scores and pain relief (visual analog scale) after surgery
articles.take_home: Adding suprascapular nerve release to rotator cuff repair did not provide extra benefits for shoulder function or pain relief compared to repair alone. Both groups improved after surgery, but there was no significant difference between them.
